mappings.rationale: Directly covers the nature and varieties of logic, including its history and philosophical underpinnings, central to both parts A and B of this section.
Aristotle founded the logic of predicates, developed syllogistic and modal logic, and is the pivotal figure in ancient logic covered in section A.1.b.
Covers the philosophical context of ancient logic including Socrates, Plato, and the Stoics, who are key contributors to ancient logic and the logic of propositions.
Socrates is identified as a precursor of ancient logic in section A.1.a, contributing to theories of language and the axiomatic method.
Plato is cited as a precursor of ancient logic in section A.1.a, contributing foundational theories of language relevant to the development of logic.
The section covers the rise of mathematical logic and the relationship between logic and the foundations of mathematics, including Frege, Cantor, and Boole's algebra of logic.
Medieval logic is a major topic (section A.2), covering Arabic contributions, the old and new logic after Aristotle's Organon, and medieval theories of language.
Section A.2.a specifically mentions Arabic contributions to medieval logic development, making Islamic philosophical traditions directly relevant.
Section A.5.a covers Indian logic, its origins in scriptural commentaries, special problems in grammar, and interest in negation.
Section A.5.b discusses Chinese logic originating in controversies between Confucianism, Taoism, and Moism, and its neglect after Neo-Confucianism.
Wittgenstein made foundational contributions to 20th-century logic and the philosophy of logic, including work on logical semantics and the limits of logic.
Russell's Logicism and theory of types are explicitly discussed in section A.4.a as a major 20th-century development in logic.
Gödel's incompleteness theorems are specifically cited in section B.1.b as a central feature and problem of the philosophy of logic.
Leibniz is cited in section A.3.b for his contributions to mathematical logic during the Enlightenment, including his general calculus of reasoning.
The philosophy of logic intersects with philosophy of science on questions of computability, logical semantics, and the limitations of logic discussed in section B.
Section B.2.b addresses problems of ontology in the philosophy of logic, including individuation and existence, which are core metaphysical concerns.
Analytic philosophy has been deeply intertwined with developments in formal logic throughout the 20th century, including work on logical semantics and alternative logics.
The Stoics are specifically credited in section A.1.d with founding the logic of propositions, a major branch of formal logic.
The section covers logic in the Renaissance (A.3.a) including the Port-Royal logic, which was deeply influenced by Cartesian methodology.
Aquinas engaged extensively with Aristotelian logic in his Scholastic method, relevant to the medieval logic developments in section A.2.
The Presocratics contributed early forms of reasoning and argumentation that set the stage for the development of formal logic discussed in section A.1.
